reviewed.by: argument+orthodoxy gates; derived from library/phil2.html (already orthodoxy-CLEAN 2026-07-04)

voiceover: You've probably sung Philippians 2 — but your English Bible hides its most explosive words in the Greek. Tucked into a prison letter is a passage that reads like a song. Scholars call it the Carmen Christi — the Song of Christ — and most think Paul is quoting something older than his own letter, sung within a generation of the cross. Word one: morphe theou — the form of God. Not a costume. The same word describes the form of a servant he truly became — so the form of God is just as real. Word two: harpagmos — one of the hardest words in the New Testament. Most now read it this way: equality with God he already had, and refused to exploit. Word three: ekenosen — he emptied himself. Here is the trap most readers miss: he did not empty himself of his deity. He emptied himself by adding humanity — remaining what he was, becoming what he was not. Word four: the name above every name — Kyrios, the Lord, the divine name itself. Paul takes Isaiah 45, where God swears to me every knee shall bow — and applies it to Jesus. This is no demigod in a crowded pantheon. It is Jesus placed inside the identity of the one God — what Richard Bauckham calls a Christology of divine identity. The worship of Jesus as God is not a late invention. It was sung at the very headwaters of the faith. The first Christians did not argue Jesus into godhood. They sang it. Jesus is Lord — to the glory of God the Father.

lines.t: like a song. Scholars call it
Not a costume. The same word
so this is just as real.
equality with God he already had —
of his deity — he emptied himself
swears “to ME every knee shall bow” —
the identity of the one God —
The worship of Jesus as God
was not a legend that grew
It was there from the start.
